author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: The `except Exception` block doesn't capture the exception, so the actual error (e.g., `ZoneInfoNotFoundError: 'Not/AZone'`) is silently dropped. If this fallback fires in production, the warning entry won't explain what went wrong or why the timezone string was rejected by `zoneinfo`. ```suggestion except Exception as e: logger.warning("snapchat_ads_invalid_account_timezone", timezone=account_timezone, error=str(e)) ```

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: There's an `info` log for currency being present, but no log when `timezone` comes back `None` from the API. If an account's timezone field is absent (newly created account, unsupported region, etc.), the code silently falls back to the pre-fix naive format and the 400s would resume with no trace in the logs to indicate why. A warning here would make the fallback observable. ```suggestion currency = account.get("currency") timezone = account.get("timezone") if currency: logger.info("snapchat_ads_account_currency", ad_account_id=ad_account_id, currency=currency) if not timezone: logger.warning("snapchat_ads_account_timezone_missing", ad_account_id=ad_account_id) return (str(currency) if currency else None, str(timezone) if timezone else None) ``` Note: If this suggestion doesn't match your team's coding style, reply to this and let me know. I'll remember it for next time!

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **SQL aliases duplicate the constants, creating a silent mismatch risk** The `AS confirmed_mrr` and `AS credits_used` aliases inside the `parse_select` string are raw literals that must exactly match `BILLING_CONFIRMED_MRR_COLUMN` / `BILLING_CREDITS_USED_COLUMN`. If either constant is ever renamed, `_billing_column_expr` will build `ast.Field(chain=["billing", ])` but the subquery will still export `` — the join succeeds but every billing cell silently returns NULL. `test_billing_columns_join_the_view_when_present` validates that the outer `SELECT` expression references `billing.confirmed_mrr`, but it does not inspect the subquery's own column list, so this mismatch would not be caught by CI. Note: If this suggestion doesn't match your team's coding style, reply to this and let me know. I'll remember it for next time!

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Bare `except Exception` silently absorbs unexpected errors** The intent ("fail closed on any error") is documented, but catching `Exception` also masks programming errors — e.g., a `TypeError` from a wrong `Database.create_for` signature after a refactor, or an `ImportError` from a missing dep — and returns `False` instead of surfacing the bug. Narrowing the catch to the expected HogQL/Django DB error types would keep the fail-closed behaviour for the cases it's designed for while still propagating genuine programming mistakes.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **HMAC key re-derived per cell in the hot loop** `mask_value` calls `_masking_key()` on every non-null value, which accesses `settings.ENCRYPTION_SALT_KEYS` and runs `.encode()` each time. For a table batch with millions of rows and several masked columns, this is O(rows × masked_columns) redundant settings lookups and allocations. The key is stable for the lifetime of the call, so it should be computed once before the loop and threaded through (e.g., as a private kwarg to `mask_value`, or by inlining the HMAC inside `mask_table_columns` after a single `_masking_key()` call at the top of the function). Note: If this suggestion doesn't match your team's coding style, reply to this and let me know. I'll remember it for next time!

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Type-erasing `str()` conversion creates hash collisions across types** The message `f"{team_id}:{value}"` stringifies `value` via `__str__`, so `mask_value(1, 42)` and `mask_value(1, "42")` both hash `b"1:42"` and produce identical digests. If a source column changes its Python type between syncs (e.g., an integer column that arrives as a string in one batch), a previously masked value would collide with its type-sibling even though it represents a semantically different piece of data. Consider encoding a type prefix (`i:`, `s:`, `f:`, etc.) or using `repr(value)` to disambiguate.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Redundant type guard after DRF field validation** By the time `validate()` runs, `ListField(child=CharField())` has already rejected anything that isn't a list of strings, so the `isinstance` guard is dead code. The identical check exists on `enabled_columns` at line 563 — this PR copies that pattern, but both are superfluous. Removing it keeps OnceAndOnlyOnce clean. ```suggestion metadata = instance.schema_metadata or {} ``` Note: If this suggestion doesn't match your team's coding style, reply to this and let me know. I'll remember it for next time!

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Sync CPU-bound work inside an `async` method** `_process_pa_table` is `async`, but `mask_table_columns` materialises an entire PyArrow column into Python objects via `to_pylist()` and then runs HMAC synchronously on every non-null value — pure CPU-bound work that blocks the event loop for the duration. For large batches with several masked columns this can stall other coroutines for a meaningful period. The adjacent `normalize_table_column_names` has the same shape, so this is consistent with existing pipeline style, but it's worth tracking both as candidates for `asyncio.to_thread` or a process-pool offload if sync latency becomes observable.

author: veria-ai[bot]
body:
**Medium: Existing synced rows stay unmasked**

When `masked_columns` changes, this path only validates and persists the setting. For schemas with already-synced data, existing Delta/table rows remain queryable in plaintext, and incremental rows that are not updated will never be rewritten, so a project user can read values after the column has been configured as masked. Trigger a reset/resync for newly masked columns, or hide/drop those columns from the exposed table until a full masked sync completes.

author: veria-ai[bot]
body:
**Medium: Masking is skipped by pipeline V3**

This call only covers the legacy `PipelineNonDLT` path. Jobs with `pipeline_version == V3` run through `posthog/temporal/data_imports/pipelines/pipeline_v3/pipeline.py`, where `_process_batch` normalizes and writes the `pa_table` without calling `mask_table_columns`, so a user can query raw values for configured masked columns on V3-enabled sources. Apply the same masking step in `PipelineV3` before schema evolution, S3 writes, Postgres batch notifications, and CDP chunk production.

author: hex-security-app[bot]
body:
**Masked warehouse columns are brute-forceable after switching to team_id-only SHA-256** `mask_value` no longer uses a server secret and now hashes `f"{team_id}:{value}"` with plain SHA-256. Because `team_id` is not secret and is readily known to any project member using this feature, an attacker who can query masked warehouse columns can offline-enumerate low-entropy values such as emails, SSNs, credit cards, or common passwords and recover the original data. This regresses the confidentiality guarantee the feature is meant to provide.
Prompt To Fix With AI ````markdown Restore a keyed construction for column masking so masked values cannot be brute-forced by users who know the team_id. If key rotation stability is required, introduce a dedicated stable masking secret or persist a per-schema/per-team masking key version rather than switching to an unkeyed hash. Update both snapshot and CDC masking paths consistently, and keep the API/docs aligned with the actual security properties. ````
--- Severity: medium | Confidence: 98% | React with 👍 if useful or 👎 if not

author: veria-ai[bot]
body:
**Medium: Masked values can be guessed offline**

`team_id` is not secret, so anyone who can query a masked warehouse column can compute `sha256(f"{team_id}:{candidate}")` for likely emails, phone numbers, card numbers, or password-like values and match them against the stored digest. Use a deterministic HMAC or peppered digest with a stable per-installation or per-team secret that is not exposed to warehouse readers, so joins still work without turning masked columns into a public dictionary oracle.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Guard duplicates a lookup that `onDrop` already performs** `dropzoneNode` is found here purely to gate the early-return, but then discarded — `onDrop()` at line 642 independently runs `values.dropzoneNodes.find((x) => x.id === values.highlightedDropzoneNodeId)` again and additionally checks `values.nodeToBeAdded`. If `nodeToBeAdded` is somehow null when this guard passes (e.g. race in a future refactor), `onDrop()` will silently no-op with no toast shown. Consider widening the guard to also assert `values.nodeToBeAdded` is set, or checking the `isCopyingNode` flag to keep the two guards consistent with what the copy flow actually needs. author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Test sends produce log entries but not metrics** The metrics path is explicitly gated on `!isTest` (line 509) to prevent test sends from appearing in a workflow's Metrics tab. The log-entries path has no equivalent guard, so a test email that bounces or triggers a complaint will write a visible log entry into the workflow's invocation logs, creating a false signal for operators debugging production issues. The `isTest` variable is in scope here (destructured from `parsedCode` on line 498) and just needs to be checked. ```suggestion // Allowlist actionId before interpolating into the [Action:…] rich-log token, // since actionId comes from the attacker-influenceable ph_id tag. const safeActionId = actionId && /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/.test(actionId) ? actionId : undefined for (const line of !isTest ? formatSesEventLogs(rec) : []) { ```

Users who see a spike in `email_failed` have no drill-down path even though the log entries exist. If the omission is intentional (e.g., these events occur before invocation context is attached), a short comment here would clarify why. author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Connection not closed on exception** `persons_conn` is opened lazily inside `ensure_person` (line 230) and closed here only on the happy path. Any exception raised during the main loop (lines 270–369) will skip this block, leaking the raw psycopg connection for the lifetime of the process. Wrapping the loop in a `try/finally` — or opening the connection with a context manager — would guarantee cleanup.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **`last_rows_consumed` docstring overstates the "slightly above" case** The docstring says the count "reaches the cap (or slightly above it, since a transaction is never split)." In practice, `pg_logical_slot_peek_binary_changes` stops once the decoded-change count becomes *≥ upto_nchanges*, and it does so after a full COMMIT boundary — so the returned count is always ≥ the cap, not just "slightly" above it: a single large transaction can push `last_rows_consumed` well past the cap. The comparison in `_read_wal_loop` (`rows_consumed < limit`) is still correct (anything ≥ limit is treated as a full page), but callers who read the docstring expecting only a small overshoot may be surprised by a page that is several multiples of `limit`. Clarifying to "at or above it" would remove the ambiguity.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **No heartbeat during between-pass flush** `_drain_and_advance_page` calls `_process_flush` (an S3 write) and `_confirm_position` (a Postgres round-trip) without firing `activity.heartbeat()`. The Temporal heartbeat timeout for this activity is 10 minutes. The last heartbeat before `_drain_and_advance_page` is called was fired via the `on_row` throttled callback (at most 30 s ago during the WAL fetch), so under normal conditions the 10-minute window is very comfortable. However, if `_process_flush` stalls — e.g., a slow S3 multipart upload or a transient network hang — the activity could be killed mid-advance with no event to keep it alive. The same gap exists for micro-flushes inside the inner `for` loop (heartbeat fires at loop-entry, before the flush), so this is consistent with existing behaviour. Adding a single `activity.heartbeat()` at the top of `_drain_and_advance_page` would close it without changing semantics.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: The `advance` function runs unconditionally without checking `partition.is_frozen`, so the freeze mechanism is non-functional. An operator calling `freeze()` via the facade expects no new trials to start and no merges to happen, but `on_trial_finished` still calls `advance(trial.partition)` — which will happily start successor trials and merge all-green enrollments regardless of the frozen flag. ```suggestion def advance(partition: Partition) -> None: """Drive the partition: start eligible trials, merge mergeable enrollments. Idempotent.""" if partition.is_frozen: return strategy = _resolve_strategy(partition) ```

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Concurrent double-merge due to missing row lock** `advance` can be driven concurrently — e.g. by two Temporal activity completions landing at the same time. Both callers will read the same enrollment via `_active_enrollments_with_all_green`, both will pass `_mergeable`, and both will call `merge_enrollment`. There is no `select_for_update()` or CAS-style update to prevent this: the result is two `MERGED` `QueueEvent` rows and two shadow-merge logs for the same enrollment. In M2 with a live guard this becomes a real double-merge. The minimal fix is to replace the unconditional `UPDATE` in `merge_enrollment` with an atomic conditional update that returns early if the row was already moved out of `ACTIVE`.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: `_strictly_ahead` is a private engine implementation detail (underscore-prefixed) being imported directly into the facade layer. The facade is the only public surface, and importing an engine internal here breaks the module boundary documented by `tach.toml`. The simplest fix is to promote the function to public in `projected_state.py`. ```suggestion from products.merge_queue.backend.engine.projected_state import strictly_ahead as _strictly_ahead ``` Note: If this suggestion doesn't match your team's coding style, reply to this and let me know. I'll remember it for next time!

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **`asyncio.create_task` without a strong reference** Per the Python docs, a `Task` returned by `create_task` should be held in a live reference until it completes, or the garbage collector may destroy it while it is still pending. The recommended pattern is to store it in a set and discard it in a done callback. This code path is unreachable in M1 (`cowboy=None`), but should be corrected before Cowboy wires in.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **`cdc_broken` removal is not atomic with the schema state reset** The loop that removes the `cdc_broken` markers runs before the `with transaction.atomic():` block. If the transaction fails (e.g., a DB connectivity blip midway through the `update(sync_type=None, should_sync=False)` call), schemas end up in a contradictory state: `cdc_broken` is gone, the schedule is already deleted, yet `sync_type=CDC` / `should_sync=True` remain. That leaves no user-visible signal about why CDC isn't running, and any future re-enable could pick up stale CDC config that the transaction was supposed to clear. Moving the loop inside the `transaction.atomic():` block resolves this — `update_sync_type_config_keys` already uses a nested `atomic()` savepoint internally, so it composes correctly under the outer transaction.

Adding a descriptive note makes it immediately clear during incident triage why the schedule is paused, rather than requiring a round-trip to application logs. ```suggestion try: pause_schedule(temporal, schedule_id=schedule_id, note="CDC marked broken — paused to prevent retrying against a missing slot/publication") except temporalio.service.RPCError as e: ``` Note: If this suggestion doesn't match your team's coding style, reply to this and let me know. I checked the codebase and `<product_upsells>` doesn't appear anywhere — not in `ee/hogai/tools/*.py`, `products/`, or the rest of the repo. The hogai tool prompts do use XML-ish tags (`<example>`, `<reminders>`, `<data_analysis_guidelines>`, etc.), but there's no established `<product_upsells>` convention to follow here, so wrapping this one bullet in a brand-new tag would introduce a one-off pattern rather than match the surrounding prompt style. Happy to adopt it if/when that convention actually lands in the prompts.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Unguarded DB insert masks non-retryable errors** `_create_failure_visibility_jobs` runs a Django ORM INSERT unconditionally inside `_handle_failure`. If that INSERT raises (e.g., a transient DB error that occurs after `schema.save()` succeeds), the exception escapes `_handle_failure`, and `_fail` never reaches the `if not info.retryable: raise NonRetryableException(…)` branch. For auth failures like `InvalidPassword`, Temporal would then retry the activity up to `maximum_attempts` times instead of stopping immediately — the exact opposite of the desired behaviour. The `_capture_non_retryable` helper already shows the correct best-effort pattern for side-effect calls in this path. ```suggestion if not self.created_jobs and (activity.info().attempt >= CDC_MAX_EXTRACTION_ATTEMPTS or not info.retryable): try: self._create_failure_visibility_jobs(friendly) except Exception: self.log.warning("cdc_failure_visibility_job_creation_failed", exc_info=True) ```

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: The two `loadAppMetricsTotals` calls have no data dependency on each other, so they can be fired in parallel with `Promise.all`. As written, the card waits for the full round-trip of the first request before even starting the second, doubling the load latency for the conversion-rate panel. ```suggestion const [conversionResponse, startedResponse] = await Promise.all([ loadAppMetricsTotals(baseRequest, timezone), loadAppMetricsTotals({ ...baseRequest, metricName: ['triggered'] }, timezone), ]) await breakpoint(10) const conversions = Object.values(conversionResponse).reduce((sum, r) => sum + r.total, 0) const started = Object.values(startedResponse).reduce((sum, r) => sum + r.total, 0) return { conversions, started } ``` Note: If this suggestion doesn't match your team's coding style, reply to this and let me know. I'll remember it for next time!

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Duplicated ordering-parse logic (OnceAndOnlyOnce)** `_list_ordering_fields` re-implements the same parse/validate/fallback logic that already lives in `_parse_signal_report_ordering` + `_apply_signal_report_ordering`: split by comma, strip leading `-`, check against `_SIGNAL_REPORT_ORDERING_FIELDS`, fall back to `_DEFAULT_SIGNAL_REPORT_ORDERING`, and append `id` as tiebreak. The only difference is the output type — this method keeps the public name while the ORM path maps to the DB column name. A shared helper that returns `list[tuple[str, bool]]` (public name + descending flag) would serve both callers and keep the validation logic in one place. As written, a future change to the allowlist or default ordering must be mirrored in two independent parsers. Note: If this suggestion doesn't match your team's coding style, reply to this and let me know. I'll remember it for next time!

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **`is_suggested_reviewer` ORDER BY is still a correlated subquery** Priority and actionability were converted to DISTINCT ON CTEs (the main optimisation), but the `is_suggested_reviewer` sort expression inlines a NOT EXISTS anti-join that runs once per row in the base set during sort. For the default ordering (`-is_suggested_reviewer,status,-updated_at`) with a user who has a github login, Postgres still evaluates this per-row EXISTS for every report the query must sort — the same pattern the PR description identifies as the original cause of slow loads. The improvement from this PR is real (priority + actionability are now bounded scans), but for teams heavy on reviewer-sorted loads the reviewer expression remains the bottleneck. A `latest_reviewer` CTE parameterised by `github_login` would apply the same fix to this key. This is a pre-existing pattern so not a regression, but it's worth a follow-up given the stated goal.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **`staging_dir_bytes` gauge is never emitted** `staging::staging_dir_bytes()` and `metrics::staging_dir_bytes()` are both implemented but there is no call site in `main.rs` (or anywhere else) that actually invokes them, so the gauge will never appear in Prometheus/metrics output. The PR description explicitly lists `staging_dir_bytes` as a delivered metric, making this dead code. To emit the gauge you'd need a periodic task (e.g., a `tokio::spawn` loop) calling `metrics::staging_dir_bytes(staging::staging_dir_bytes(&staging_dir).await as f64)` on startup, or at minimum a one-shot call alongside the sweep. Note: If this suggestion doesn't match your team's coding style, reply to this and let me know. I'll remember it for next time!

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Sweep exits early on `file_type` or `next_entry` error, leaving remaining entries un-swept** The `?` on `entries.next_entry().await?` and `entry.file_type().await?` propagates any I/O error out of the function immediately. If the filesystem returns an error for a single entry mid-sweep (e.g., a TOCTOU deletion), the rest of the directory is never scanned and `removed` is returned as a partial count. The caller in `main.rs` treats this as a non-fatal warn, so startup can continue, but leaked entries after the failing one are silently left behind. Wrapping the per-entry operations in their own `match`/`unwrap_or` (as the remove step already does) would keep the sweep best-effort end-to-end.

author: veria-ai[bot]
body:
**Medium: OAuth resource indicator is not bound to the MCP server**

`resource` comes from the MCP server's protected-resource metadata and is later sent in the authorize/token requests, while the resulting bearer token is still sent to `installation.url`. A malicious custom MCP server can advertise an unrelated API as the resource, have the user authorize that audience, and then receive that API token during tool sync or proxying; only accept resource identifiers whose origin is bound to the installed MCP server (or otherwise restrict this to curated template metadata).

author: hex-security-app[bot]
body:
**Untrusted OAuth resource indicator can exfiltrate third-party access tokens** For custom MCP installs, `discover_oauth_metadata()` copies the protected-resource metadata's `resource` value straight into `installation.oauth_metadata`, and `oauth_resource()` later sends that value in both the authorize and token requests. The resulting bearer token is then forwarded to `installation.url` during proxy/tool sync without verifying that the requested resource belongs to the MCP server. A malicious MCP server can therefore trick a user into authorizing a token for an unrelated API audience and then receive that third-party token through the MCP proxy.
Prompt To Fix With AI ````markdown Bind OAuth resource indicators to the installed MCP server before persisting or using them. For custom installs, only accept a `resource` whose origin matches the MCP server origin (or another narrowly defined safe relationship), otherwise ignore/reject it. Apply the same validation before using stored metadata in authorize/token flows, and add tests covering malicious cross-origin resource values to ensure tokens for unrelated audiences are never requested and then forwarded to the MCP server. ````
--- Severity: medium | Confidence: 91% | React with 👍 if useful or 👎 if not Fixed in `77fec5f9`.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: Replace `assert` with an explicit check. Python assertions are no-ops when run with `-O` / `PYTHONOPTIMIZE`, so if `parse_select` ever returns an `ast.SelectUnionQuery`, the subsequent `query.select` assignment would raise `AttributeError` at query-execution time with no useful context. A plain `if/raise` is clear regardless of optimisation flags. ```suggestion per_action_totals = [0] * len(actions) for team in _project_environment_teams(ctx.team): query = parse_select( "SELECT 1 FROM events WHERE timestamp >= now() - toIntervalDay({days})", placeholders={"days": ast.Constant(value=CONVERSIONS_LOOKBACK_DAYS)}, ) if not isinstance(query, ast.SelectQuery): raise TypeError(f"evaluate_conversions: expected SelectQuery, got {type(query)}") ```

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **`optimize_budget` always crashes: 7-tuple unpacked as 5** `decompose_and_curves` transforms `run.curves` from 5-tuples `(channel, sp, inc, lo, hi)` into 7-tuples `(job_id, team_id, channel, sp, inc, lo, hi)` before returning. `optimize_budget` receives that updated `run`, so when `_equalize_marginal_roi` does `for channel, spend_point, incremental, _lo, _hi in run.curves:` it attempts to unpack a 7-element tuple into 5 variables — `ValueError: too many values to unpack` on every real execution. The budget optimisation step never completes; the last op in the pipeline (`persist_run`) is also never reached. The unit tests don't catch this because they call `_interp` / `_marginal_return` directly with raw point lists, never constructing a `MmmRun` with stamped curves.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Write endpoint uses read scope** The `mmm_calibrations` POST action (the only write endpoint in this POC) declares `required_scopes=["marketing_analytics:read"]`. A personal API token explicitly scoped to `marketing_analytics:read` — e.g. one issued for a read-only integration — can therefore mutate calibrations even though the token holder intended to restrict it to reads. The staff gate in `_assert_mmm_access` is a separate check and doesn't substitute for the correct scope; a staff user with a deliberately scoped read-only token would still be able to write. The scope for the POST branch should be `marketing_analytics:write` (or a combined list for the shared endpoint, split by `request.method`).

author: veria-ai[bot]
body:
**Medium: Read-only access can update MMM calibrations**

`mmm_calibrations` handles `POST` by saving `config.mmm_channel_calibrations`, but the action declares `marketing_analytics:read`. In this viewset that scope also drives API-token and access-control checks, so a staff user or token with only read access can replace the calibration priors and change future model output; split the read/write actions or derive `marketing_analytics:write` for `POST`.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Duplicated hash pipeline: OnceAndOnlyOnce violation** The `git ls-tree | grep | grep -v | sort | sha256sum` pipeline is copy-pasted verbatim into two separate steps — once at line ~416 (applied to `$MERGE_BASE` in `turbo-discover`) and again here (applied to `HEAD`). The comment even flags this explicitly: "Must match the merge-base computation … exactly." If the grep patterns are updated in one place but not the other, the migration-set key computed on master will silently diverge from the one computed for PRs, causing every PR to get a cache miss on the new key and fall back to the slower SHA key. The regression would be functional but invisible — CI would still pass, just slower, and hard to attribute to a specific change. Extracting the pipeline into a small shell script or a composite action that both steps invoke would make drift structurally impossible.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Misleading comment: the `||` fallback is never reachable** The comment states that `schema_migrations_key is '' only when the migration hash could not be computed, in which case the SHA key is used.` In reality, both `schema_migrations_key` and `schema_cache_key` are set to empty string together (when `MERGE_BASE` is not found), and the step's own guard condition (`schema_cache_key != ''`) skips the entire `actions/cache/restore` step when either is empty. This means when the step runs, `schema_migrations_key` is always non-empty, so `|| schema_cache_key` is a dead fallback and the comment describes a scenario that cannot occur. The comment (present in all three restore sites) should be removed or corrected to avoid misleading future readers about what the `||` does.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Non-atomic read-check-write loses participants under concurrency.** Two `forward_posthog_code_followup_activity` calls for different users arriving at the same thread simultaneously will each read the same `participant_slack_user_ids` list, each pass the membership check, and the second `.save()` will silently overwrite the first — dropping one user from the list, so they won't see the task on their Home tab. Use an atomic SQL `array_append` with a `WHERE NOT … = ANY(…)` guard instead. ```suggestion def add_participant(self, slack_user_id: str) -> None: """Record a Slack user as having @-mentioned the bot in this thread (idempotent).""" if not slack_user_id: return from django.db.models.expressions import RawSQL updated = ( SlackThreadTaskMapping.objects.filter(id=self.id) .exclude(participant_slack_user_ids__contains=[slack_user_id]) .update( participant_slack_user_ids=RawSQL( "array_append(participant_slack_user_ids, %s::varchar)", [slack_user_id], ) ) ) if updated: self.participant_slack_user_ids = [*self.participant_slack_user_ids, slack_user_id] ```

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Blocking Slack API call inside a 3-second event callback window.** `publish_home_tab` runs `gather_involved_tasks` (two DB round-trips) and then `slack.client.views_publish` (an outbound HTTP call) inside the same synchronous request that Slack expects to receive a `200` within 3 seconds. Under any load spike or slow Slack API response, Slack will retry the event delivery, resulting in duplicate `views.publish` calls and confusing tab flicker for the user. The standard mitigation is to return `200` immediately and schedule the publish in a background task or Temporal activity.

author: veria-ai[bot]
body:
**Low: Slack mrkdwn injection**

A Slack user who can start or reply to a coding-agent task can include Slack control characters in the task text so another participant's Home tab renders attacker-controlled mrkdwn, such as a forged link label. Escape task-derived strings before putting them into `mrkdwn` blocks; at minimum centralize Slack escaping for `&`, `<`, and `>` and apply it to `task.title`, repository names, stage/error text, and any link labels.

author: Copilot
body: The JSDoc now says it hides the “Properties/Device top-level selector”, but the UI still labels this control “Match by” (with Properties/Device options). Also, the code uses the term `bucketingIdentifier`, so “bucketing identifier” is clearer/consistent than “bucketing type”.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: The reason string hardcodes "above baseline" regardless of the sign of `relative_delta_percentage`. When `current_value < baseline_value` the delta is negative, so the message reads "was -25.00% above baseline" — factually incorrect and visible to users in alert event history and notification payloads. ```suggestion direction = "above" if relative_delta_percentage >= 0 else "below" reason = ( f"Current value {current_value} was {abs(relative_delta_percentage).quantize(Decimal('0.01'))}% " f"{direction} baseline {baseline_value.quantize(Decimal('0.000001'))}; " f"threshold is {threshold_percentage}%." ) ```

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **N+1 DB query in `get_destination_types`** `get_destination_types` fires a separate `HogFunction.objects.filter()` query for every `BillingAlertConfiguration` in the response. The frontend loads up to 500 alerts in one call (`billingAlertsList(organizationId, { limit: 500 })`), so the list endpoint would issue up to 500 extra DB queries per page load. Consider pre-aggregating template IDs via a single fan-out query passed through serializer context so the template IDs can be resolved without a per-row hit.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Race condition in `consecutive_failures` increment** `alert.consecutive_failures += 1` is a Python read-modify-write. If two workers (e.g., two Temporal activity retries) concurrently handle an error for the same alert, both read the same value, both increment to the same value, and one write wins — the alert loses a failure count. This can delay (or in theory prevent) the transition to the `BROKEN` state. Replace with a database-level increment using `F("consecutive_failures") + 1` combined with `select_for_update()` or an equivalent atomic update.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **No RESOLVED event when a SNOOZED alert's threshold resolves** `_event_kind` only emits `RESOLVED` when `alert.state == FIRING`. If an alert transitioned `FIRING → SNOOZED` (threshold breached during snooze window) and the threshold then resolves while snoozed or after the snooze expires, `alert.state` is `SNOOZED`, so the second condition is never true. The state silently moves to `NOT_FIRING` and no RESOLVED notification is dispatched. Depending on the desired UX, either expand the RESOLVED condition to include `SNOOZED`, or document that RESOLVED is intentionally suppressed after a snooze.

Fixed in `038fcd619f3` — the error path now locks and reloads the alert row before incrementing `consecutive_failures`, so stale workers cannot clobber the latest failure count or delay the `BROKEN` transition.

Fixed in `038fcd619f3` — `SNOOZED` alerts now emit `RESOLVED` when the threshold clears, with a regression test covering the state transition.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Silent notification drop on fire→resolve→re-fire within the same evaluation date** `dispatch_billing_alert_event` guards against duplicate dispatch with `locked_event.notification_sent_at is not None`. However, `evaluate_and_record_billing_alert` uses `update_or_create` keyed on `(alert, kind=FIRING, evaluation_date)`. Because evaluation_date changes only once per billing day, if an alert fires, resolves, and fires again within the same billing day, the second `update_or_create` overwrites the existing FIRING event in-place (with a fresh `state_before=NOT_FIRING → state_after=FIRING` transition) but leaves `notification_sent_at` from the original dispatch untouched. `dispatch_billing_alert_event` then silently returns 0, and the state machine's NEW transition never reaches the user's destination. This means a user monitoring a billing spike that briefly dips and returns within the same 24-hour billing window will miss the second notification. The failure is invisible to the caller — `event_should_dispatch` returns `True` and the function is called, but it exits silently via the guard. Moving the `notification_sent_at` idempotency check out of `dispatch_billing_alert_event` and relying solely on the `BillingAlertDelivery.idempotency_key` unique constraint for at-most-once delivery would fix this, as each new logical transition would generate a fresh idempotency key.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: The `order` parameter is interpolated directly into the f-string SQL without any validation or parameterization. ClickHouse does not support parameterised `ORDER BY` direction, so the literal must be embedded, but there is no guard ensuring it is one of `"ASC"` or `"DESC"`. A future caller that accidentally passes a non-literal value (e.g. derived from config or a flag) could inject arbitrary SQL. A cheap assertion at function entry eliminates this class of mistake. ```suggestion """Deduped (other_id, target_id, first_seen) identity edges with timestamp in [start, end). Capped at config.max_identity_edges to bound the in-memory union-find. ``order`` ('ASC' or 'DESC', a trusted literal — never request input) decides which edges survive truncation: anchor edges keep the most recent (DESC, closest to window end), eval edges keep the earliest (ASC, each orphan's first post-window merge). """ assert order in ("ASC", "DESC"), f"order must be 'ASC' or 'DESC', got {order!r}" query = f""" ```

author: graphite-app[bot]
greptile-apps[bot]
body: When a new note is created, `loadNotebooks()` is called with the current page rather than resetting to page 1. Under the default `-created_at` ordering, a freshly created note always lands at the top of page 1. If the user happens to be on page 2+ when clicking "New note," the list reloads page 2 and the new note is only visible in the side panel — not in the table. Replacing the direct `loadNotebooks` call with `setPage(1)` resets the page and triggers the reload through the existing listener chain, so the new note appears in the table immediately. ```suggestion createNoteSuccess: ({ createdNote }) => { if (!createdNote) { return } posthog.capture(AccountsEvents.NoteCreated, { notebook_short_id: createdNote.short_id }) actions.selectNotebook(createdNote.short_id, { autofocus: 'end' }) actions.setPage(1) }, ```

author: arthurdedeus
body: Good catch — fixed in 68e92c9719f by resetting to page 1 in `createNoteSuccess` (via `setPage(1)`), with a regression test that creates from page 2 and asserts the reset.

author: arthurdedeus
body: Good catch — fixed in 68e92c9719f by resetting to page 1 in `createNoteSuccess` (via `setPage(1)`), with a regression test that creates from page 2 and asserts the reset.

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: The positive-path assertion uses substring matching, so `"app.example.com"` would also match against a malformed stored value like `"https://bad.app.example.com"`. Since the goal of the test is to confirm that the field is persisted exactly as supplied, an equality check is both stricter and clearer about intent. ```suggestion self.assertEqual(app.allowed_origins, "https://app.example.com https://www.example.com") ```

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: **Unrelated snapshot change bundled in PR** This file adds `RetentionQuery` (v2) and `StickinessQuery` (v2) checks to the query-upgrade workflow snapshot — nothing to do with OAuth or the test coverage described in the PR description. Was this committed accidentally (e.g., from an uncommitted branch state), or is it an intentional dependency of some CI test run? Note: If this suggestion doesn't match your team's coding style, reply to this and let me know. I'll remember it for next time!

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body: The `team.get("id")` call silently returns `None` when a team object from the Linear API is missing its `id` field, which would add `None` to `linear_team_ids`. If `linear_team_id` were ever `None` (e.g., an explicit null from request data), this would allow the validation to pass. Since `id` is a required primary key for every Linear team, a missing `id` should be treated as a malformed response and fail loudly rather than quietly poisoning the set. Prefer `team["id"]` to raise a `KeyError` on malformed data. ```suggestion linear_team_ids = {team["id"] for team in self.list_teams()} ``` **Rule Used:** For primary key fields or other critical required ... ([source](https://app.greptile.com/posthog-org-19734/github/PostHog/posthog/-/custom-context?memory=be55dab6-5161-4694-bfc8-99df4255406a)) **Learned From** [PostHog/posthog#45266](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/45266#discussion_r2705381394)

author: cat-ph
body: we'll have other issues if the response shape changes from linear, plus this is meant to prevent user-error, IMO better here to allow it if this fails

author: greptile-apps[bot]
body:
Fair enough — since this validation is specifically meant to catch user error (bad team IDs), being lenient about unexpected API response shapes makes sense. If Linear changes their response format, the downstream `create_issue` call will fail anyway and surface the problem clearly. No objection to keeping `.get("id")` here.

Tip: You can customize Greptile's behavior for this repo with [`.greptile/rules.md` and `.greptile/config.json`](https://www.greptile.com/docs/code-review/custom-standards).

author: chatgpt-codex-connector[bot]
body:
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Validate against all Linear teams, not just first page**

For workspaces with more than 50 Linear teams, this validation can reject a legitimate `team_id` because `list_teams()` calls `teams { nodes { id name } }` without paginating, and Linear list responses return only the first 50 results by default (https://linear.app/developers/pagination). The issue creation mutation would otherwise accept an accessible team outside that first page, so this new pre-check turns valid requests into `Invalid Linear team_id`; paginate through all teams or validate the specific team id directly.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
